examples.title: 0/1 Knapsack - Bottom-Up DP
examples.description: Standard dynamic programming solution using 2D table
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Knapsack01(values, weights, n, W)
BEGIN
    // Create DP table with (n+1) x (W+1) dimensions
    DECLARE DP[n+1][W+1]
    
    // Initialize base cases: 0 items or 0 capacity
    FOR w FROM 0 TO W DO
        DP[0][w] = 0
    END FOR
    
    FOR i FROM 0 TO n DO
        DP[i][0] = 0
    END FOR
    
    // Fill DP table row by row
    FOR i FROM 1 TO n DO
        FOR w FROM 1 TO W DO
            IF weights[i-1] <= w THEN
                // Maximum of including or excluding current item
                DP[i][w] = max(DP[i-1][w], 
                              values[i-1] + DP[i-1][w-weights[i-1]])
            ELSE
                // Cannot include item, carry forward previous value
                DP[i][w] = DP[i-1][w]
            END IF
        END FOR
    END FOR
    
    RETURN DP[n][W]  // Maximum value for n items and capacity W
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm builds solution incrementally. For each item i and capacity w, we decide: either don't include the item (DP[i-1][w]) or include it (values[i-1] + DP[i-1][w-weights[i-1]]). Taking maximum of both gives optimal value. Time: O(nW), Space: O(nW).

examples.title: 0/01 Knapsack - Space Optimized
examples.code:
def knapsack_01(values, weights, W):
    n = len(values)
    # Space-optimized 1D DP array
    dp = [0] * (W + 1)
    
    # Process each item
    for i in range(n):
        # Traverse in REVERSE order to prevent item reuse
        for w in range(W, weights[i] - 1, -1):
            # Either keep previous value or include current item
            dp[w] = max(dp[w], values[i] + dp[w - weights[i]])
    
    return dp[W]

# Example usage
values = [10, 10, 12, 18]
weights = [2, 3, 4, 5]
W = 8
print(f"Maximum value: {knapsack_01(values, weights, W)}")
# Output: 28 (items with weights 3 and 5)
examples.explanation: By using reverse traversal, each item is considered only once because dp[w-weights[i]] still contains value from previous iteration (previous item). Forward traversal would cause the same item to be counted multiple times.

examples.title: Subset Sum - Boolean DP
examples.description: Find if any subset sums to target S
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SubsetSum(arr[], n, target)
BEGIN
    // Create DP table (n+1) x (target+1)
    DECLARE DP[n+1][target+1]
    
    // Base case: empty subset can form sum 0
    FOR i FROM 0 TO n DO
        DP[i][0] = TRUE
    END FOR
    
    // Base case: no elements cannot form positive sum
    FOR sum FROM 1 TO target DO
        DP[0][sum] = FALSE
    END FOR
    
    // Fill table
    FOR i FROM 1 TO n DO
        FOR sum FROM 1 TO target DO
            IF arr[i-1] <= sum THEN
                // Include or exclude current element
                DP[i][sum] = DP[i-1][sum] OR DP[i-1][sum-arr[i-1]]
            ELSE
                // Cannot include, element too large
                DP[i][sum] = DP[i-1][sum]
            END IF
        END FOR
    END FOR
    
    RETURN DP[n][target]
END

// To find the actual subset:
ALGORITHM FindSubset(arr[], n, target, DP)
BEGIN
    IF DP[n][target] = FALSE THEN
        RETURN "No subset found"
    
    DECLARE subset = []
    sum = target
    
    FOR i FROM n DOWN TO 1 DO
        IF sum == 0 THEN BREAK
        
        // If value came from above, item was excluded
        // Otherwise, item was included
        IF i == 0 THEN BREAK
        IF sum >= arr[i-1] AND DP[i-1][sum-arr[i-1]] == TRUE THEN
            subset.append(arr[i-1])
            sum = sum - arr[i-1]
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    RETURN subset
END
examples.explanation: DP[i][sum] is TRUE if either we can form 'sum' without element i-1, or we can form 'sum-arr[i-1]' using first i-1 elements. The backtracking algorithm traces which decisions led to TRUE at DP[n][target].

examples.title: 0/1 Knapsack - Memoization (Top-Down)
examples.description: Recursive approach with caching for subproblem results
examples.explanation: Memoization stores results in a 2D array. Before computing, we check if the result is already cached. This ensures each subproblem is solved only once. The recursion naturally follows the optimal substructure property. Time: O(nW), Space: O(nW) for memo table + O(n) recursion stack.
